Our Audiences// 01

Our Audiences

Core Demographic

The City of Lafayette communicates with and serves everyone who lives, works, visits, and does business here. Our community members each play a role in shaping our City’s identity and future.

As a public service organization, we are committed to transparent, inclusive, and accessible engagement that reflects the diversity of our community.

Key Segments
  • Residents
  • Local Businesses
  • Visitors
  • Community Partners
  • Media
  • Regional & State Agencies
Brand Position// 02 + 03

Brand
Position

"We weave the daily life and culture of Lafayette into our communications to create a two-way network between the City and the community."

Small Town. Big Lives. Open Minds.

A brand position defines how we want our community to think and feel about the City of Lafayette. It is our internal north star — a shared statement that guides the tone, priorities, and personality behind every communication we send.

Personality// 04

Personality

Vibe and Spirit

A unique blend of artsy and outdoorsy. Friendly, neighborly, and diverse, with a down-to-earth sensibility. We strive to communicate like the neighbor you actually want to talk to: approachable, authentic, and welcoming.

Voice Pillars

Engaging, creative, and big-thinking yet approachable. Inclusive and reflective of our eclectic community.

Writing Do's// 05

Writing Do's

City Protocol

Use clear, concise, and plain language. Be conversational yet professional. Lead with what matters most. Use visual elements that reinforce the City’s brand. Always link to official City resources for details and transparency

Digital Presence

Keep social media posts short, clear and impactful. When communicating directly with residents, use “Lafayette” instead of “the City” to sound more approachable. Include clear calls to action when appropriate.

Social Media Examples
✓ Do This

"Lafayette's fall cleanup day is this Saturday, March 24! Join your neighbors at Waneka Lake from 9am–noon. Gloves and bags provided. 🍂 lafayetteco.gov/cleanup"

✓ Do This

"Road closure alert: South Public Road between Emma Street and Baseline Road will be closed Mon–Wed for utility work. Plan your route at lafayetteco.gov/Roads"

✓ Do This

"We want to hear from you! Share your thoughts on the proposed Downtown Comprehensive Plan at Tuesday's open house — 6pm at City Hall. All are welcome."

✗ Avoid This

"The City of Lafayette Department of Public Works hereby announces that the aforementioned corridor will be temporarily inaccessible to vehicular traffic."

✗ Too Formal — Avoid

"Pursuant to Section 4.2 of the municipal code, residents are hereby notified of the scheduled maintenance operations which shall commence at 0700 hours."

▶ Rewrite as: "Crews will be working on [location] starting at 7am on [date]. Thanks for your patience!"
✗ No CTA — Avoid

"The City of Lafayette held its annual budget meeting last Tuesday. Various topics were discussed including infrastructure and public safety."

▶ Always end with a link, action, or next step for the reader.
✗ Too Long — Avoid

"It is with great pleasure that the City of Lafayette Communications Department announces that applications for the Community Advisory Board are now being accepted through the month of April, with all community members who meet the qualifications welcome to apply by visiting our website."

▶ Aim for under 280 characters on social. Use a link for details.
Quick Copy Templates

Copy and adapt these starter templates for your next post, alert, or event announcement.

News Flash

[TOPIC]: [What happened/is happening] in [location]. [Key detail or impact]. For more info: lafayetteco.gov/[page]

Keep under 140 characters for social
Event Post

[Event name] is [date] at [time], [location]. [1 sentence on what to expect]. All are welcome — [link or contact]

Include date, time, location — always
Alert / Urgent

ALERT: [Issue] affecting [location/service]. [What residents should do now]. Updates at lafayetteco.gov or call [number].

Lead with action — what should residents do?
Social Engagement

[Hook or question]. [1–2 sentences of context]. Tell us: [CTA or question] 👇 [emoji optional]

End with a question to invite engagement
Writing Style Guide Website Style Guide
Perspective// 06 + 07

Perspective

First Person (Preferred for community engagement)

Use first person (“we,” “our”) for casual, community-oriented communication. This approach feels welcoming and conversational. Best for social media and event invitations.

"We're excited to invite you to our next town hall."
Third Person (More formal and official)

Use third person for formal, official, or record-based communication. Best for news flashes, press releases, reports and official documents, Council communications and policy or educational content.

"The City of Lafayette has published the 2024 budget."
Interactive Tool // Palette Studio

Color Control

Select a palette from the dropdown to explore the City's official color system. Click any swatch to copy its HEX value. Use the lock to hold colors while exploring variations.

Note: All color themes are available to every department — you are not limited to your department's named palette. For example, the Sustainability theme can be used by any department, not only Sustainability.

Click any swatch to see RGB, CMYK, and HEX values. Click a value to copy it.

Interactive Tool // Sandbox

Typography
Lab

Preview type combinations and check contrast ratios before publishing. Fonts are available to download in the Asset Downloads section.

Headline Font Family Body Font Family Background Color
Font Color
Accent Font Family
Brand Script Accent (Short Only) Accent Size (Readable Range)

LAFAYETTE

Franklin Gothic is our primary workhorse. It should represent 90% of our written communications for maximum legibility.

Small Town. Big Lives.
CONTRAST RATIO: 21.0 (PASS)
Recommended Font Pairings
✓ Recommended — Standard
Libre Franklin
+ Libre Franklin body — Our core workhorse. Maximum legibility for all audiences.
✓ Recommended — Impact
Bebas Neue
+ Libre Franklin body — Bold headlines for social posts, banners, and alerts.
✓ Recommended — Expressive
Mr Dafoe
+ Libre Franklin body — Script accent only for taglines and event flyers. Never for body copy.
✗ Avoid This Pairing
Roboto Mono
+ Roboto Mono body — Double-mono reads as code, not civic communication. Use mono only for labels and metadata.

Lafayette Logo
Blueprint // 08

Logos

DO: Approved Usage
DO: For Dark Assets

DON'T: Distort
Logo Do's & Don'ts
✗ Don't
Don't Stretch or Squish Always scale the logo proportionally. Never adjust width or height independently.
✗ Don't
Don't Recolor Only use approved versions: standard RGB, white reverse, or CMYK. No custom colors.
✗ Don't
Don't Place on Low Contrast Use RGB on white/light. Use white reverse on dark. Ensure sufficient contrast for legibility.
✗ Don't
Don't Add Effects No drop shadows, glows, gradients, outlines, or filters applied to the logo mark.
✗ Don't
Don't Use Too Small Minimum print: 1 inch wide. Minimum digital: 80px wide. Below this, legibility is lost.
✓ Do
Always Use Clear Space Maintain clear space equal to the height of the "L" in Lafayette on all sides of the logo.
Brand Imagery // 09

Brand Imagery

Lafayette’s story is told through its people, places, and creative energy. The following image categories guide how we visually represent our community in a way that feels real, vibrant, and distinctly Lafayette.

Category 01: People

Authentic, diverse and candid community members that reflects real life in Lafayette. Avoid staged scenarios or generic stock imagery.

Category 02: Scenery

Breathtaking views of the Flatirons and wide-open spaces that define the Lafayette landscape.

Category 03: Events

Vibrant community gatherings that highlight connection and the eclectic local culture.

Category 04: Public Art

Showcasing the creative spirit of the city through eclectic and energizing public works.

How to install a font: Click the link below to open Google Fonts. Click the blue "Download family" button in the top right. Unzip the downloaded folder, then double-click each .ttf file and click "Install Font" (Mac) or "Install" (Windows). The font will then appear in Word, Canva, and all other apps.
